alter_rebbe.label: The Alter Rebbe — Founder of Chabad (1772–1812)

events.title: Birth of the Baal Shem Tov (18 Elul)
events.desc: Rabbi Yisrael ben Eliezer is born on 18 Elul in Okopy, Podolia (modern Ukraine), to Eliezer and Sarah. According to tradition, he was orphaned at a very young age. He grows up in the forests of Podolia, developing a profound attachment to nature and the Divine.

events.title: Early Orphan Years of the Baal Shem Tov
events.desc: After his parents die, the young Yisrael is taken in by the community. As a child he serves as an assistant in a cheder (Jewish school), then as a shamash. Traditions record that he would often disappear into the forests to commune with God, developing the spiritual sensitivity that would later revolutionize Jewish life.

events.title: Baal Shem Tov Marries Leah Rachel
events.desc: The Baal Shem Tov marries Leah Rachel, sister of Rabbi Gershon Kitover of Brody. Rabbi Gershon initially looks down on him, thinking him an ignorant laborer. Only years later does he learn of his brother-in-law's spiritual greatness. The couple moves to the Carpathian mountains.

events.title: The Hidden Years in the Carpathians
events.desc: For nearly two decades, the Baal Shem Tov lives a hidden life as a simple lime-digger in the Carpathian mountains. He studies Torah in secret and achieves the highest spiritual levels. His wife Leah Rachel supports the family while he digs clay and spends his nights in Torah study.

events.title: Baal Shem Tov Studies Under Ahiya HaShiloni
events.desc: According to Chabad tradition, the Baal Shem Tov's primary heavenly teacher was Ahiyah HaShiloni (the prophet who lived from Moses until Elijah). Through mystical encounters, the BeShT receives the deepest secrets of the Torah and the foundations of his revolutionary approach to Divine service.

events.title: Baal Shem Tov Reveals Himself
events.desc: At age 36, on his birthday (18 Elul), the Baal Shem Tov publicly reveals himself as a master. He begins teaching that God's presence fills all of creation, that joy is a path to the Divine, and that sincere prayer by a simple person is as precious as the learning of a great scholar. This marks the founding of the Chassidic movement.

events.title: Baal Shem Tov's Teaching Circle in Medzhibozh
events.desc: The Baal Shem Tov settles in Medzhibozh, which becomes the center of the nascent Chassidic movement. Hundreds of disciples gather to hear his teachings. He emphasizes: love of every Jew ('Ahavat Yisrael'), prayer with intense feeling (kavanah), the sparks of holiness in all things, and the evil in sadness and self-flagellation.

events.title: Baal Shem Tov's Letter to Rabbi Gershon Kitover
events.desc: The Baal Shem Tov writes a famous letter to his brother-in-law Rabbi Gershon Kitover in the Land of Israel, describing a soul-ascent on Rosh Hashanah to the heavenly Academy. He recounts meeting Moshiach and asking when he will come; Moshiach answers: 'When your wellsprings spread outward.' This letter is considered foundational to the Chassidic movement's missionary character.

events.title: Birth of the Alter Rebbe (18 Elul)
events.desc: Rabbi Shneur Zalman of Liadi is born on 18 Elul 5505 in Liozna, Belarus — the same calendar date as the Baal Shem Tov's birthday, 47 years later. Already as a child he is recognized as extraordinarily gifted. By age 8 he has mastered Talmud; by 12, local rabbis cannot answer his questions.

events.title: Baal Shem Tov's Inner Circle — the Chevre Kaddisha
events.desc: The Baal Shem Tov gathers an inner circle of disciples known as the 'Chevra Kaddisha' (Holy Brotherhood). Among them are Rabbi Dov Ber of Mezeritch (who will succeed him), Rabbi Pinchas of Koretz, Rabbi Nachum of Chernobyl, and Rabbi Yaakov Yosef of Polnoye — the first person to publish Chassidic teachings in a book.

events.title: 'Toldos Yaakov Yosef' — First Chassidic Book
events.desc: Rabbi Yaakov Yosef of Polnoye publishes Toldot Yaakov Yosef, the first printed book of Chassidic teachings, transmitting hundreds of teachings in the name of the Baal Shem Tov. Its publication ignites a fierce controversy with the Mitnagdim (opponents of Chassidism) in Lithuania and Poland.

events.title: First Ban Against the Chassidim Issued
events.desc: The rabbinic leadership of Brody issues the first formal ban (cherem) against the Chassidim, forbidding people from associating with them or reading their books. This marks the beginning of the great controversy between Chassidism and its opponents (Mitnagdim), which will last for decades.

events.title: Passing of the Baal Shem Tov (6 Sivan — Shavuot)
events.desc: The Baal Shem Tov passes away on Shavuot, 6 Sivan 5520, in Medzhibozh. Before his death, he gathers his disciples and blesses them. He dies in the middle of study, with a smile on his face. His student Rabbi Yaakov Yosef writes that the world lost its brightest light. The Maggid of Mezeritch succeeds him.

events.title: Rabbi Dov Ber — the Maggid of Mezeritch — Assumes Leadership
events.desc: Rabbi Dov Ber of Mezeritch becomes the second leader of the Chassidic movement following the Baal Shem Tov's passing. Under his leadership, Chassidism expands dramatically across Eastern Europe. He attracts many of the greatest scholars of the generation, including the young Shneur Zalman of Liadi.

events.title: Alter Rebbe Travels to Study Under the Maggid
events.desc: At age 18, Rabbi Shneur Zalman faces a choice: travel to Vilna to study under the Vilna Gaon, or to Mezeritch to study under the Maggid. He reasons: 'I know how to learn Torah; I need to learn how to pray.' He chooses Mezeritch, where he quickly becomes one of the Maggid's outstanding students.

events.title: Alter Rebbe Chosen to Write New Shulchan Aruch
events.desc: At the request of his teacher the Maggid, and Rabbi Menachem Mendel of Vitebsk, the 20-year-old Alter Rebbe is tasked with composing a new code of Jewish law that would be clear and accessible. This becomes Shulchan Aruch HaRav — a monumental work that takes decades to complete.

events.title: Alter Rebbe Marries Rebbetzin Sterna Sarah
events.desc: Rabbi Shneur Zalman marries Rebbetzin Sterna Sarah (Sterna), who becomes an extraordinary partner in his mission. She manages their household with great wisdom and supports his Torah work. Many stories in Chabad tradition highlight her piety and her understanding of Chassidus.

events.title: Maggid Assigns Alter Rebbe to Write New Shulchan Aruch
events.desc: The Maggid formally commissions Rabbi Shneur Zalman to produce an updated, authoritative code of Jewish law incorporating Sephardic and Ashkenazic traditions. The resulting Shulchan Aruch HaRav becomes the definitive halachic authority for Chabad communities.

events.title: Great Vilna Ban Against Chassidim
events.desc: The Vilna Gaon (Rabbi Eliyahu of Vilna) issues a sweeping ban against Chassidim, excommunicating them from the Jewish community. Chassidic books are burned in public. The Alter Rebbe is personally targeted. Despite this, Chassidism continues to spread rapidly throughout Russia, Poland, and Ukraine.

events.title: Passing of the Maggid of Mezeritch (19 Kislev)
events.desc: Rabbi Dov Ber of Mezeritch passes away on 19 Kislev. His passing fractures the Chassidic movement into many courts. The Alter Rebbe returns to Belarus and, over the following years, develops Chabad as a distinct school emphasizing intellectual understanding (ChaBaD — Chochma, Bina, Da'at) as the gateway to emotional transformation.

events.title: Birth of the Mitteler Rebbe (9 Kislev)
events.desc: Rabbi Dovber Schneuri, the future second Rebbe of Chabad, is born on 9 Kislev 5533 in Liozna. From childhood he demonstrates an extraordinary capacity for Chassidic contemplation. He will later be known for delivering discourses that last hours, during which he would fall into deep mystical trances.

events.title: Alter Rebbe Begins Writing the Tanya
events.desc: Rabbi Shneur Zalman begins composing the Tanya, collecting and refining answers he had given to thousands of disciples' spiritual questions over the years. The work represents his synthesis of Kabbalah, Chassidus, and practical guidance for the average person's inner life. He works on it for over 20 years before publishing.

events.title: Alter Rebbe and Rabbi Menachem Mendel Travel to the Holy Land
events.desc: The Alter Rebbe and Rabbi Menachem Mendel of Vitebsk begin a journey to the Land of Israel with 300 followers. They reach as far as Mogilev before the Alter Rebbe turns back, heeding his teacher's instruction to remain in Russia and strengthen Chassidism there. Rabbi Menachem Mendel continues and establishes a Chassidic community in the Holy Land.

events.title: Second Major Ban Against Chassidim
events.desc: A second, even harsher ban against Chassidim is issued in Vilna. The Alter Rebbe, along with Rabbi Menachem Mendel of Vitebsk, travels to Vilna to seek a meeting with the Vilna Gaon and resolve the dispute. The Gaon refuses to receive them. The Alter Rebbe returns deeply saddened, and the schism between Chassidim and Mitnagdim deepens.

events.title: Alter Rebbe Establishes Court in Liozna
events.desc: The Alter Rebbe establishes his primary court in Liozna, which becomes a major center of Chabad learning. He delivers regular Chassidic discourses (maamarim) and holds private audiences (yechidus) with thousands of followers. The Chabad movement grows substantially during this period.

events.title: Birth of the Tzemach Tzedek (29 Elul)
events.desc: Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneersohn, the future third Rebbe of Chabad, is born on 29 Elul 5549 in Liozna to Rabbi Shachna and Devorah Leah, daughter of the Alter Rebbe. His mother passes away when he is just three years old. He is raised primarily by his maternal grandfather, the Alter Rebbe himself.

events.title: Alter Rebbe Moves to Liadi
events.desc: The Alter Rebbe relocates his court to Liadi, a larger town in Belarus where he would spend the next two decades. Liadi becomes one of the most important centers of Jewish life in Russia. Thousands of Chassidim make pilgrimages there for Rosh Hashanah and other holidays.

events.title: First Printed Edition of the Tanya Published
events.desc: The Tanya (Likkutei Amarim) is published in Slavuta in its first printed edition, 5556. It is immediately recognized as a masterwork. The Alter Rebbe writes in his introduction that the Tanya is a 'compendium of counsel' for the 'intermediate person' (beinoni) — not just for tzaddikim but for every Jew striving for spiritual life.

events.title: Third Renewal of the Ban Against Chassidim
events.desc: Following the passing of the Vilna Gaon, the Mitnagdim renew the ban against Chassidim with even greater force. In Minsk, a decree is issued that Chassidim should be expelled from the community. This leads directly to an informant filing charges against the Alter Rebbe with the Tsarist authorities.

events.title: First Arrest — Alter Rebbe Imprisoned (19 Kislev)
events.desc: On 15 Tishrei 5559 (1798), the Alter Rebbe is arrested by order of Tsar Paul I and transported to St. Petersburg. He is held in the Peter and Paul Fortress, interrogated on 22 counts of alleged sedition, including sending money to the Ottoman Empire (which was actually charitable donations to the Holy Land). He is held for 53 days.

events.title: Liberation of the Alter Rebbe (19 Kislev)
events.desc: On 19 Kislev 5559 (December 1798), the Alter Rebbe is released from prison without charges. The date coincides with the yahrzeit of the Maggid of Mezeritch and becomes the greatest Chabad holiday — Yud-Tes Kislev, the 'Rosh Hashanah of Chassidus.' The Alter Rebbe emerges from prison and delivers the Chassidic discourse 'Atah Achat' — affirming God's unity.

events.title: Second Edition of the Tanya Published
events.desc: A second, expanded edition of the Tanya is published, including new sections. The Alter Rebbe continues adding letters and epistles that will eventually form the fourth and fifth sections of the Tanya (Iggeret HaKodesh and Kuntres Acharon). The work grows into five distinct volumes.

events.title: Second Arrest of the Alter Rebbe
events.desc: Following continued denunciations, the Alter Rebbe is arrested a second time and brought to St. Petersburg. He is again held briefly and released. According to Chabad tradition, during his interrogation the Tsar himself was impressed by the Rebbe's knowledge and bearing. He was released within weeks.

events.title: Alter Rebbe Sends Emissaries Throughout Russia
events.desc: Following his liberations, the Alter Rebbe greatly expands his network of emissaries (mevakrim) who travel throughout Russia, Poland, and Ukraine to support Chassidic communities. These emissaries collect funds for the poor, teach Chassidus, and maintain connections between the movement's members.

events.title: Alter Rebbe's Responsa on Shechita Controversy
events.desc: A major controversy erupts over the Chassidic practice of using sharpened knives for ritual slaughter (shechita). The Alter Rebbe writes responsa defending the Chassidic practice, arguing from first principles of halacha. His responsa are considered landmark documents in both halachic and polemical literature.

events.title: Alter Rebbe's Discourse on the Essence of Chassidus
events.desc: The Alter Rebbe delivers a series of discourses explaining the philosophical foundations of Chabad Chassidus to a gathering of leading rabbis. He articulates the distinction between Chabad — which uses intellectual contemplation to achieve emotional transformation — and other Chassidic schools that begin with emotional arousal.

events.title: Alter Rebbe's Igeret HaTeshuva Published
events.desc: The third section of the Tanya, Iggeret HaTeshuvah (Epistle of Return), is published. It provides a detailed, psychological analysis of the process of teshuvah (repentance/return) that differs from the harsh penitential practices then common in Eastern Europe, emphasizing heart-transformation over bodily afflictions.

events.title: Alter Rebbe Flees East Ahead of Napoleon
events.desc: As French forces advance, the Alter Rebbe leads a massive refugee caravan eastward. Thousands of Chassidim follow him. The journey is arduous; many fall ill. The Alter Rebbe himself becomes increasingly weakened. He travels in a wagon through the Russian winter, delivering Chassidic teachings to his followers along the way.

events.title: Napoleon Invades Russia — Alter Rebbe's Decision
events.desc: When Napoleon's Grande Armée invades Russia, the Alter Rebbe faces a fateful choice. He publicly supports the Tsar, reasoning that while Napoleon's rule might improve Jews' material conditions, the resulting secularization would destroy their spiritual lives. He tells his son: 'If Napoleon wins, the Jews will prosper materially but spiritually be lost. If the Tsar wins, Jews will suffer materially but remain spiritually intact.'

events.title: Passing of the Alter Rebbe (24 Tevet)
events.desc: Rabbi Shneur Zalman of Liadi passes away on 24 Tevet 5573 (December 27, 1812) in the village of Pyena near Haditch, Ukraine. He was 67 years old. His last discourse, delivered just before his death, was on the verse 'The soul of the man is God's candle.' He is buried in Haditch, which becomes a major Chabad pilgrimage site. His son Rabbi Dovber succeeds him.

events.title: Mitteler Rebbe Assumes Leadership in Lubavitch
events.desc: Rabbi Dovber Schneuri assumes leadership of Chabad and establishes his court in Lubavitch (a town in the Smolensk region, Russia). The name 'Lubavitch' becomes synonymous with Chabad from this point forward. He is known for delivering extraordinary discourses lasting many hours, during which he would fall into states of profound mystical absorption.

events.title: Schism — Rabbi Aharon of Staroselye Breaks Away
events.desc: Rabbi Aharon HaLevi of Staroselye, the Alter Rebbe's closest disciple, breaks from Rabbi Dovber and establishes his own Chabad-influenced court. He argues that the Alter Rebbe's path emphasized emotional transformation through intellect, while Rabbi Dovber focuses too much on the intellect itself. This is one of Chabad's first internal controversies.

events.title: Mitteler Rebbe Publishes His First Major Work
events.desc: Rabbi Dovber publishes Kuntres HaHitpaalut (Tract on Ecstasy), a detailed examination of authentic vs. false spiritual emotion in Divine service. This work distinguishes between genuine ecstasy (hitpaalut) born of deep contemplation and superficial emotional excitement. It becomes a classic of Chassidic literature.

events.title: Mitteler Rebbe Expands Lubavitch Yeshiva
events.desc: Rabbi Dovber significantly expands the yeshiva in Lubavitch, attracting hundreds of students from across Russia and Poland. He introduces a structured curriculum combining Talmud, halacha, and Chassidus, establishing the template that Tomchei Temimim would later institutionalize.

events.title: Chabad Pioneers Settle in Hebron, Israel
events.desc: A group of Chabad Chassidim led by Rabbi Menachem Mendel of Vitebsk's disciples immigrate to Hebron in the Land of Israel. They establish one of the earliest Chabad communities outside Russia, beginning what will become a significant Chabad presence in the Holy Land.

events.title: Mitteler Rebbe's Torah Or Discourses
events.desc: Rabbi Dovber delivers extensive maamarim (Chassidic discourses) on the deepest mysteries of Kabbalistic thought, particularly on the nature of the Ein Sof (the Infinite) and its relationship to creation. His discourses are noted for their extraordinary intellectual rigor and mystical depth.

events.title: Mitteler Rebbe Arrested Briefly
events.desc: Rabbi Dovber is briefly arrested by Russian authorities on charges similar to those leveled against his father. He is released quickly. The arrest reinforces the Chabad practice of always having advocates at the Russian court in St. Petersburg to handle such situations.

events.title: Passing of the Mitteler Rebbe (9 Kislev)
events.desc: Rabbi Dovber Schneuri passes away on 9 Kislev 5587 (November 1826) in Niezhin. He is 53 years old. His passing occurs just 10 days before the anniversary of his father's liberation (19 Kislev). In Chabad, both dates are celebrated together as the 'Festival of Redemption.' He authored over 40 major works of Chassidic philosophy.

events.title: Tzemach Tzedek Becomes Third Rebbe
events.desc: After a period of doubt (during which he tries to defer to others), Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneersohn accepts the leadership of Chabad. He becomes one of the most prolific authors in Chabad history, eventually writing 48 volumes of Chassidic teachings (Ohr HaTorah) and an encyclopedic halachic compendium (Shut Tzemach Tzedek).

events.title: Birth of the Maharash (2 Iyar)
events.desc: Rabbi Shmuel Schneersohn, the future fourth Rebbe of Chabad, is born on 2 Iyar 5594 (1834) in Lubavitch. He is the seventh son of the Tzemach Tzedek. Known for his extraordinary warmth and ability to relate to all types of people, he will become famous for his motto about 'going over' rather than 'under.'

events.title: Torah Or and Likkutei Torah Published
events.desc: The Tzemach Tzedek edits and publishes Torah Or (1837) and Likkutei Torah (1848) — the Alter Rebbe's collected discourses on the weekly Torah portions. These two works, together with the Tanya, form the triad of fundamental Chabad texts studied to this day. Their publication is a monumental contribution to Jewish literature.

events.title: Russian Government Forces Attendance at Haskalah Schools
events.desc: Tsar Nicholas I launches a campaign to 'modernize' Jewish education, mandating attendance at government schools that teach secular subjects including Russian language. The Tzemach Tzedek strongly opposes these schools, seeing them as vehicles for assimilation and conversion. He instructs his followers to resist them.

events.title: St. Petersburg Rabbinical Conference
events.desc: The Russian government convenes a rabbinical assembly in St. Petersburg to discuss 'reform' of Jewish education. The Tzemach Tzedek attends but wages a determined battle against all proposals that would dilute religious education. He is briefly imprisoned for his intransigence. He returns home victorious, having prevented the worst reforms.

events.title: Tzemach Tzedek's Famous Response to His Name
events.desc: When asked in St. Petersburg why his halachic compendium is called 'Tzemach Tzedek' (Sprout of Righteousness — a name for Moshiach), he replies: 'Because I wish to be a Tzemach Tzedek — a Sprout of Righteousness who will cause justice to flourish.' The name becomes his title for posterity.

events.title: Tzemach Tzedek Sends Emissaries to Defend Chabad Schools
events.desc: In response to the Russian government's campaign to close traditional chederim, the Tzemach Tzedek organizes a network of advocates and emissaries to lobby local governors and protect Chabad educational institutions. This establishes a pattern of political advocacy that Chabad leaders would employ repeatedly.

events.title: Tzemach Tzedek Writes His Son Letters on Shlichus
events.desc: The Tzemach Tzedek writes to his son (the Maharash) about the meaning of shlichus (being an emissary of God). He articulates that a shliach acts with the full authority of the one who sends him — a principle that will underpin the entire Chabad emissary system a century later.

events.title: Birth of the Rebbe Rashab (20 Cheshvan)
events.desc: Rabbi Shalom Dovber Schneersohn is born on 20 Cheshvan 5621 (1860) in Lubavitch, the fifth and youngest son of the Maharash. From early childhood he demonstrates exceptional emotional depth and intellectual precision. At age 8, he has a private audience with his grandfather the Tzemach Tzedek, which profoundly shapes his spiritual development.

events.title: Maharash Becomes Fourth Rebbe
events.desc: Rabbi Shmuel Schneersohn assumes leadership of Chabad. He is known for an approach of tackling challenges directly and unapologetically — famously expressed in his maxim: 'The world says — if you can't go under, go over. I say — l'chatchila ariber (to begin with, go over)' — meaning one should aim high from the outset rather than compromising.

events.title: Passing of the Tzemach Tzedek (13 Nissan)
events.desc: Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneersohn passes away on 13 Nissan 5626 (1866), one day before Passover eve. He is 77 years old and has led Chabad for 39 years. He leaves behind 48 volumes of Chassidic teachings, a massive halachic compendium, and tens of thousands of devoted followers. The movement he leaves is the largest Chassidic dynasty in Russia.

events.title: Maharash Travels to Western Europe for Jewish Advocacy
events.desc: The Maharash makes an extraordinary journey to Western Europe — including Paris and Berlin — to advocate on behalf of Russian Jews and to seek support from Western Jewish communities for his followers. This is among the first such diplomatic trips by a Chabad Rebbe.

events.title: Maharash Issues Responsa on Torah Study Methods
events.desc: The Maharash publishes key responsa clarifying the Chabad approach to Torah study — emphasizing depth of understanding (iyun) over breadth, and the essential role of Chassidic philosophy (Chassidus) alongside traditional Talmud study. These responsa define Chabad's educational philosophy for generations.

events.title: Maharash Establishes Major Charitable Networks
events.desc: The Maharash organizes an extensive charitable network (tzedakah system) throughout Russia to provide for impoverished Chassidim, particularly during times of famine and government oppression. This network demonstrates Chabad's commitment to material as well as spiritual welfare.

events.title: Birth of the Frierdiker Rebbe (12 Tammuz)
events.desc: Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn is born on 12 Tammuz 5640 (1880) in Lubavitch. From early childhood he accompanies his father the Rebbe Rashab everywhere and is groomed for leadership. He is deeply devoted to Chassidic study and practice from his earliest years.

events.title: Passing of the Maharash (13 Tishrei)
events.desc: Rabbi Shmuel Schneersohn passes away on 13 Tishrei 5643 (1882) at age 48 — relatively young. His passing leaves the movement in grief and transition. Despite his shorter tenure, he significantly strengthened Chabad institutions and authored important Chassidic works. His son Rabbi Shalom Dovber succeeds him.

events.title: Rebbe Rashab Assumes Leadership
events.desc: Rabbi Shalom Dovber Schneersohn assumes the leadership of Chabad at age 22, following his father's passing. His leadership spans nearly four decades — one of the most tumultuous periods in Russian-Jewish history — encompassing pogroms, World War I, and the Russian Revolution.

events.title: Rebbe Rashab's Early Discourses on the Inner Torah
events.desc: In his early years as Rebbe, Rabbi Shalom Dovber delivers a series of discourses on the inner dimensions of Torah — exploring the relationship between the mystic revelation of Sinai and the daily experience of the Jewish person. These early maamarim establish the deep philosophical character of his leadership.

events.title: Rebbe Rashab Founds Yeshiva in Tomchei Temimim
events.desc: The Rebbe Rashab begins preparatory work for establishing a new type of yeshiva — one that would combine rigorous Talmudic study with systematic study of Chassidic philosophy. He envisions graduates who are both halachic scholars and deeply committed Chassidim: 'temimim' (complete ones).

events.title: Rebbe Rashab's Letter Against Zionism
events.desc: The Rebbe Rashab writes a famous letter vigorously opposing political Zionism, arguing that a Jewish State established by secular means — without Torah and without awaiting Divine redemption — would be spiritually dangerous and would undermine the authentic Jewish mission. This position will define Chabad's relationship with Zionism for decades.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe Begins Work at Tomchei Temimim
events.desc: At age 17, the young Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak begins working closely with his father the Rebbe Rashab in establishing Tomchei Temimim. He serves as a key administrator and teacher, deeply involved in shaping the curriculum and culture of the new yeshiva. This experience prepares him for his future leadership role.

events.title: Herzl Convenes First Zionist Congress
events.desc: Theodor Herzl convenes the First Zionist Congress in Basel, Switzerland, declaring the goal of establishing a Jewish state in Palestine. The Rebbe Rashab responds with concern, fearing that secular nationalism will draw Jews away from Torah. He begins articulating Chabad's counter-vision of religious national renewal grounded in Torah observance.

events.title: Tomchei Temimim Yeshiva Founded in Lubavitch
events.desc: On 15 Elul 5657 (1897), the Rebbe Rashab formally opens Yeshivat Tomchei Temimim in Lubavitch. The yeshiva has a revolutionary curriculum: in the mornings, students study Talmud and halacha; in the afternoons, they study Chassidic philosophy intensively. The first class has 30 students. Within years it grows to hundreds.

events.title: Rebbe Rashab's Famous Letter on Mikveh
events.desc: The Rebbe Rashab writes a landmark letter emphasizing the centrality of mikveh (ritual immersion) to Jewish spiritual life, particularly for men immersing before prayer — a Chassidic practice that the Mitnagdim had criticized. He articulates mikveh as a transformative spiritual act, not merely a legal requirement.

events.title: Rebbe Rashab Begins Hemshech Samech-Vov (5666)
events.desc: The Rebbe Rashab begins delivering the Hemshech Samech-Vov — a vast, continuous series of Chassidic discourses (ultimately spanning years and thousands of pages) exploring the deepest mysteries of Kabbalistic cosmology. It addresses questions of divine contraction (tzimtzum), infinite light, and the purpose of creation with unprecedented philosophical depth.

events.title: Birth of the Lubavitcher Rebbe (11 Nissan)
events.desc: Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson is born on 11 Nissan 5662 (April 18, 1902) in Nikolaev, Ukraine, to Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Schneerson (a renowned kabbalist and later a martyr of Soviet persecution) and Rebbetzin Chana Yanovsky. He is a direct descendant of the Tzemach Tzedek through his father's line.

events.title: Rebbe's Father — Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Schneerson
events.desc: The Rebbe's father, Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Schneerson, was the Rabbi of Yekaterinoslov (later Dnepropetrovsk) and a brilliant kabbalist. He was eventually arrested by the Soviet authorities in 1939 and exiled to Kazakhstan, where he passed away in 1944. The Rebbe's relationship with his father deeply influenced his worldview.

events.title: Kishinev Pogrom — Rebbe Rashab Responds
events.desc: The devastating Kishinev pogrom (Easter 1903, 49 Jews killed, hundreds injured) shocks the Jewish world. The Rebbe Rashab organizes relief efforts and writes about Jewish self-defense, arguing that Jews have the right and obligation to defend themselves against attackers — a position with significant halachic implications.

events.title: Russian Revolution of 1905 — Rebbe Rashab's Response
events.desc: The Russian Revolution of 1905 and the subsequent wave of pogroms (Kishinev, Odessa, others) devastate Russian Jewry. The Rebbe Rashab works tirelessly to provide relief and maintain Jewish communal life. He warns against Jewish involvement in revolutionary politics, fearing the consequences for the Jewish community.

events.title: Tomchei Temimim Graduates First Class of Shluchim
events.desc: Tomchei Temimim graduates its first cohort of rabbis trained under the combined curriculum of Talmud and Chassidus. These graduates fan out as teachers and spiritual leaders across Russia. The Rebbe Rashab declares: 'My students should be soldiers of God, prepared for all missions.'

events.title: Rebbe Rashab's Discourse on the Mission of Chabad
events.desc: The Rebbe Rashab delivers a famous discourse (known as 'Kol HaTzome') outlining the spiritual mission of the Chassidic movement: to spread the inner dimension of Torah ('pnimiyut haTorah') to every corner of the world and to every type of Jew. This discourse lays the philosophical foundation for Chabad's universal outreach mission.

events.title: Birth of the Future Rebbe in Nikolaev (11 Nissan)
events.desc: Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson is born on 11 Nissan 5662 (April 18, 1902) — correction: actually 1902. His father Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Schneerson is a prominent kabbalist. Even as a child, Rabbi Menachem Mendel demonstrates extraordinary intellectual gifts and a deep attachment to Torah.

events.title: Rebbe Rashab Opens Tomchei Temimim Branches
events.desc: Branches of Tomchei Temimim are opened in multiple cities across Russia, dramatically expanding access to Chabad education. By this time, the yeshiva has trained hundreds of students and the Chabad movement has a network of dedicated, well-educated emissaries throughout the Russian empire.

events.title: Rebbe Rashab Writes Against Agudat Yisrael
events.desc: When Agudat Yisrael is founded as the umbrella organization for Orthodox Jewry, the Rebbe Rashab expresses reservations about Chabad joining any pan-Orthodox political movement, insisting on Chabad's independence. He writes that Chabad's unique mission is best fulfilled through its own distinct path, not through coalitions.

events.title: World War I — Tomchei Temimim Disrupted
events.desc: World War I devastates Jewish communities in the Pale of Settlement. Tomchei Temimim is forced to relocate multiple times as the German and Russian armies advance and retreat. Despite enormous hardships, the Rebbe Rashab and his disciples maintain the yeshiva throughout the war, moving it from city to city.

events.title: Lubavitch Abandoned — Court Moves to Rostov
events.desc: As German forces advance into Russia, the Rebbe Rashab is forced to leave Lubavitch permanently. The town — the spiritual home of Chabad for 102 years — is abandoned. The court relocates to Rostov-on-Don in southern Russia. This marks the end of the 'Lubavitch era' of physical residence in the original hometown.

events.title: Russian Revolution — Chabad Under Bolshevik Threat
events.desc: The Bolshevik Revolution transforms Russia overnight. The new Communist government is militantly atheist and anti-religious. Within months, religious institutions are under threat. The Rebbe Rashab begins organizing resistance to religious persecution, understanding that Communism poses an existential threat to Jewish life in Russia.

events.title: Rebbe Rashab Writes About Communism's Threat to Judaism
events.desc: The Rebbe Rashab writes prophetic letters warning that Communism represents a more dangerous enemy to Judaism than any before it — not because it kills Jews physically but because it seeks to destroy their souls. He calls on his followers to give their lives rather than violate Torah under Communist pressure.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe Becomes Sixth Rebbe
events.desc: Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn accepts the nesius (leadership) of Chabad in Rostov immediately following his father's passing. He faces an immediate crisis: the Bolshevik government is systematically destroying Jewish religious life. He begins organizing underground resistance within days of assuming leadership.

events.title: Passing of the Rebbe Rashab (2 Nissan)
events.desc: Rabbi Shalom Dovber Schneersohn passes away on 2 Nissan 5680 (March 21, 1920) in Rostov-on-Don. He is 60 years old. His 38-year leadership was marked by extraordinary literary productivity (over 2,000 maamarim), the founding of Tomchei Temimim, and courageous confrontation of modernity's challenges to traditional Judaism. His son Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak succeeds him.

events.title: Underground Yeshivos and Mikvaot Established
events.desc: The Frierdiker Rebbe organizes an extraordinary underground network throughout the Soviet Union: secret yeshivos operating in private homes, hidden mikvaot (ritual baths) constructed in basements and forests, and covert religious schools for children. This operation involves thousands of people at enormous personal risk.

events.title: Soviet Campaign Against Religion Intensifies
events.desc: The Soviet government launches an intensive campaign to eradicate religion, closing synagogues, confiscating Jewish community property, and arresting rabbis. The Yevsektsiya (Jewish Section of the Communist Party) is particularly zealous in persecuting traditional Judaism. The Frierdiker Rebbe's network operates in increasingly dangerous conditions.

events.title: Future Rebbe Meets the Frierdiker Rebbe in Rostov
events.desc: The young Rabbi Menachem Mendel visits the Frierdiker Rebbe in Rostov for the first time. The encounter proves transformative. The Frierdiker Rebbe, recognizing his extraordinary gifts, draws him close and eventually chooses him to marry his daughter. The young man studies under the Frierdiker Rebbe intensively.

events.title: Chabad's Soviet Underground Network at Peak
events.desc: By 1924, the Frierdiker Rebbe's underground network includes dozens of secret yeshivos, hundreds of teachers, and thousands of students throughout the USSR. Funds flow in from outside the Soviet Union — from American and European Chassidim — to support this massive operation. The network operates in constant danger of exposure.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe Moves to Leningrad
events.desc: The Frierdiker Rebbe relocates from Rostov to Leningrad (St. Petersburg), where he can be closer to diplomatic contacts and better manage the underground operation. From Leningrad he directs an intricate network of Jewish education and religious life spanning the entire USSR.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe Establishes Secret Printing Operation
events.desc: Unable to print Jewish books openly, the Frierdiker Rebbe establishes underground printing operations to produce Chassidic texts and Jewish educational materials. Books are printed secretly and distributed through covert networks. Several people involved in this operation are eventually arrested.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe's Network Members Arrested
events.desc: The Soviet secret police (OGPU) begins systematically arresting members of the Chabad underground network. Teachers, rabbis, and yeshiva students are imprisoned, sent to gulags, and in some cases executed. Despite these losses, the network continues operating, with new people recruited to fill the arrested members' roles.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe Arrested by Soviet Secret Police
events.desc: On 15 Sivan 5687, the Frierdiker Rebbe is arrested by OGPU agents at his home in Leningrad. He is charged with counter-revolutionary religious activity and initially sentenced to death — later commuted to 10 years in a labor camp in Siberia, then to 3 years in the remote town of Kostroma. His arrest triggers a worldwide diplomatic campaign for his release.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe Expelled to Kostroma, Then Abroad
events.desc: After serving a brief exile in Kostroma, the Frierdiker Rebbe is permitted to leave the Soviet Union. He travels to Riga, Latvia — then to various European cities — continuing to direct his underground network from outside Soviet borders. He maintains contact through an intricate system of couriers and coded messages.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe's Diary of Imprisonment
events.desc: Following his release, the Frierdiker Rebbe writes a detailed account of his arrest, interrogation, and imprisonment. The account describes how he refused to recant his religious activities or sign false confessions, even under extreme pressure. He tells his interrogators: 'The only authority I acknowledge is the Almighty God.' This document inspires generations of Jewish activists.

events.title: Future Rebbe Helps Organize Frierdiker Rebbe's Defense
events.desc: When the Frierdiker Rebbe is arrested by the Soviet OGPU, Rabbi Menachem Mendel plays a key role in organizing the response — coordinating communications, reaching out to diplomatic contacts, and supporting the defense effort. His organizational abilities become evident to the Chabad community for the first time.

events.title: Liberation of the Frierdiker Rebbe (12-13 Tammuz)
events.desc: On 12-13 Tammuz 5687, following extraordinary diplomatic pressure, the Frierdiker Rebbe is released from prison and his sentence commuted. He immediately resumes his activities. The date — 12-13 Tammuz — becomes a major Chabad holiday, Yud-Beis/Yud-Gimmel Tammuz, celebrating not only personal liberation but the triumph of Torah over oppression.

events.title: World Jewry Rallies for Frierdiker Rebbe's Release
events.desc: News of the Frierdiker Rebbe's arrest spreads worldwide. Jewish communities, organizations, and individuals — including non-Chassidim and non-observant Jews — petition governments to intervene. The German and Latvian governments apply diplomatic pressure on the Soviet Union. American Jewish organizations organize protests. The case becomes an international human rights issue.

events.title: Marriage to Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka (14 Kislev)
events.desc: Rabbi Menachem Mendel marries Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka Schneersohn in Warsaw on 14 Kislev 5689. The match is arranged by the Frierdiker Rebbe himself. The date — 14 Kislev — falls shortly before Yud-Tes Kislev (the Chabad holiday). Their partnership, though childless, is a model of devotion; the Rebbe later says she was the most important person in his life.

events.title: Rabbi Menachem Mendel Marries Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka (14 Kislev)
events.desc: Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson (the future Rebbe) marries Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka Schneersohn, the Frierdiker Rebbe's second daughter, on 14 Kislev 5689 in Warsaw. The date is celebrated annually in Chabad worldwide. The couple has no children but maintains an extraordinarily devoted and close relationship until her passing in 1988.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe's First Visit to the United States
events.desc: The Frierdiker Rebbe makes his first visit to the United States, spending several months in New York. He is received with enormous fanfare. He assesses the state of American Jewish life and begins laying groundwork for future Chabad presence in America. He raises substantial funds for his ongoing underground operations in Russia.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe Visits the Holy Land
events.desc: The Frierdiker Rebbe visits the Land of Israel, where he is welcomed by the existing Chabad communities in Jerusalem, Hebron, and Jaffa. He strengthens these communities and begins planning for expanded Chabad presence in the Holy Land. The visit generates enormous religious excitement.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe Moves Court to Warsaw
events.desc: The Frierdiker Rebbe moves from Riga to Warsaw, Poland, which has a large Chassidic community and better communications infrastructure. Warsaw becomes the new center of Chabad activity. The Frierdiker Rebbe sets up his yeshiva, press, and administrative headquarters there.

events.title: Rebbe Studies Engineering in Berlin
events.desc: Rabbi Menachem Mendel studies at the University of Berlin, pursuing engineering and science. He is already a profoundly learned Torah scholar but seeks secular knowledge as well, reflecting his belief that Torah and science are complementary. When the Nazis rise to power, he leaves Germany and continues his studies in Paris.

events.title: Rebbe Studies at École Polytechnique in Paris
events.desc: In Paris, Rabbi Menachem Mendel studies at the prestigious École Polytechnique, continuing his engineering studies. He and Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka live a quiet life, attending the Frierdiker Rebbe's court (which is in Warsaw). The Rebbe's mastery of advanced science will later enable him to engage brilliantly with questions of science and religion.

events.title: Tomchei Temimim Branches Established in Europe
events.desc: The Frierdiker Rebbe establishes multiple branches of Tomchei Temimim in Europe — in Warsaw, Riga, and other cities — to replace the yeshivos that the Soviet government had closed in Russia. These yeshivos serve both as educational institutions and as centers for training future emissaries to the USSR.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe Warns About Nazi Germany
events.desc: As Nazi persecution of German Jews intensifies, the Frierdiker Rebbe warns publicly about the existential threat Hitler poses to European Jewry. He works to help Jews emigrate and urges Jewish leaders worldwide to take the Nazi threat seriously — warnings that are, tragically, largely ignored.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe Organizes Emigration Aid
events.desc: Following Kristallnacht (November 1938), the Frierdiker Rebbe intensifies his efforts to help Jews escape Nazi Germany and occupied territories. He uses his connections with diplomats and Jewish organizations to facilitate emigration, particularly for rabbis, Chassidim, and their families.

events.title: Dramatic Escape from Warsaw via Berlin
events.desc: The Frierdiker Rebbe's rescue from Warsaw involves a remarkable route: he travels through Nazi-occupied territory with transit papers obtained through American diplomatic channels. The operation, coordinated by Chabad emissaries in America and diplomatic contacts, is one of the most dramatic rescue stories of the early Holocaust period.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe in Riga Then Stockholm
events.desc: After escaping Poland, the Frierdiker Rebbe stays briefly in Riga, then Stockholm, Sweden. From Sweden he writes to his followers still trapped in Europe, encouraging them and continuing to direct what remains of his underground network. He prepares to travel to the United States.

events.title: Nazi Invasion of Poland — Frierdiker Rebbe Trapped in Warsaw
events.desc: When Germany invades Poland on September 1, 1939, the Frierdiker Rebbe is in Warsaw. Bombs fall on the city. He refuses initial opportunities to flee, unwilling to abandon his community. Over the following weeks, through a complex rescue operation involving American diplomats and Chabad connections worldwide, he is evacuated from Nazi-occupied Warsaw.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe Arrives in America (9 Adar II)
events.desc: Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn arrives in New York City on 9 Adar II 5700 (March 19, 1940). His immediate declaration — 'America is not different' (America iz nisht andersh) — challenges the prevalent assumption that religious observance was impossible in America. He immediately begins building Jewish institutions in the New World.

events.title: Headquarters at 770 Eastern Parkway Established
events.desc: The Frierdiker Rebbe establishes his headquarters at 770 Eastern Parkway in Crown Heights, Brooklyn — a Victorian mansion purchased for the purpose. '770' becomes one of the most famous addresses in the Jewish world. It houses his office, a synagogue (beis midrash), and later a yeshiva. Replicas of 770 are eventually built in dozens of cities worldwide.

events.title: Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch Founded
events.desc: The Frierdiker Rebbe founds Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch (Central Organization for Jewish Education) as the educational arm of Chabad-Lubavitch. It publishes Jewish educational materials in English, Hebrew, and other languages, and eventually oversees Chabad's worldwide educational activities. Its publications reach millions of Jews annually.

events.title: Rebbe Appointed Head of Merkos and Kehos
events.desc: The Frierdiker Rebbe appoints his son-in-law Rabbi Menachem Mendel as head of Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch (educational arm) and Kehos Publication Society. These appointments place the Rebbe at the center of Chabad's institutional life and allow him to demonstrate his extraordinary organizational and intellectual capacities.

events.title: Rebbe and Rebbetzin Escape to America (28 Sivan)
events.desc: Rabbi Menachem Mendel and Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka arrive in New York on 28 Sivan 5701 (June 23, 1941), having navigated an extraordinarily complex escape from Nazi-occupied Europe. The Rebbe immediately joins his father-in-law's institutions. In Chabad, 28 Sivan is observed as the date 'when the world's light arrived in America.'

events.title: Tomchei Temimim Reestablished in Brooklyn
events.desc: The flagship Chabad yeshiva, Tomchei Temimim, is reestablished on American soil in Brooklyn. Students include Holocaust refugees who arrived with the Rebbe, as well as American-born young men — an unprecedented combination that helps bridge European Chassidic tradition with American Jewish life.

events.title: Kehos Publication Society Founded
events.desc: The Frierdiker Rebbe establishes Kehos Publication Society, which becomes one of the world's most prolific publishers of Jewish books. Over subsequent decades it publishes the complete works of all seven Chabad Rebbes, countless educational volumes, and Jewish holiday books — eventually comprising thousands of titles in dozens of languages.

events.title: Machne Israel Founded
events.desc: Machne Israel is established as the social welfare and religious outreach arm of Chabad-Lubavitch. It takes on responsibility for providing matzot to Jews worldwide (including behind the Iron Curtain), supporting poor Chassidic families, and strengthening Jewish observance in communities lacking resources.

events.title: Rebbe's Letter-Writing Campaign Begins
events.desc: Rabbi Menachem Mendel begins what will become one of the most extraordinary correspondence relationships in Jewish history. He responds personally to thousands of letters from Jews worldwide — on matters of halacha, Chassidus, personal dilemmas, and existential questions. Over his lifetime, he will write tens of thousands of letters, published in 27 volumes of Igrot Kodesh.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe's Annual Letters on Jewish Holidays
events.desc: The Frierdiker Rebbe begins the practice of issuing annual public letters on major Jewish holidays — Rosh Hashanah, Passover, and others. These letters, distributed to thousands of Jews worldwide, address contemporary challenges to Jewish life and inspire Jewish commitment. The practice continues under the Rebbe.

events.title: Kfar Chabad Established in Israel
events.desc: A dedicated Chabad village — Kfar Chabad — is established near Tel Aviv in the Land of Israel, providing a home base for Chabad activities in the Holy Land. It eventually becomes a thriving community with schools, yeshivos, agricultural operations, and a major Chabad printing house.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe Sends Emissaries to DP Camps
events.desc: As World War II ends, the Frierdiker Rebbe sends Chabad emissaries to the displaced persons (DP) camps in Germany, Austria, and Italy. These emissaries establish Tomchei Temimim yeshivos in the camps, providing religious education and community to Holocaust survivors. Thousands of Jews reconnect with their heritage through these efforts.

events.title: Operation to Rescue Torah Scrolls from Europe
events.desc: The Frierdiker Rebbe organizes efforts to rescue Torah scrolls and holy books from Nazi-devastated European Jewish communities. Chabad emissaries in the DP camps collect religious artifacts and transport them to safety. Many of these rescued items eventually reach the United States or Israel.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe Advocates for Israel
events.desc: As the United Nations debates the partition of Palestine, the Frierdiker Rebbe's position is nuanced: while he has reservations about Zionism as a secular movement, he writes that Jews have an absolute right to the Land of Israel by divine promise. He prays for the safety of the Jewish community in the Holy Land.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe Responds to Israel's Establishment
events.desc: When the State of Israel is declared on 14 Iyar 5708 (May 14, 1948), the Frierdiker Rebbe writes that while the establishment of a Jewish state is momentous, Jews must ensure that the state will be guided by Torah values. He expresses particular concern for the religious character of the new state.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe's Health Declines
events.desc: The Frierdiker Rebbe's health deteriorates significantly in his final year. He suffers from multiple ailments including complications from earlier strokes. Despite being largely confined to his room, he continues receiving visitors, writing letters, and directing Chabad activities with undiminished intellectual sharpness until his final months.

events.title: Frierdiker Rebbe's Final Maamer — Basi L'Gani
events.desc: On 10 Shevat 5710, the Frierdiker Rebbe's last Chassidic discourse — Basi L'Gani — is published. Based on the verse 'I have come to my garden,' it speaks of God's return to dwell in this world through human effort. The Rebbe (who succeeds him) will deliver discourses elaborating this text every year on this date for over 40 years.

events.title: Passing of the Frierdiker Rebbe (10 Shevat)
events.desc: Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn passes away on 10 Shevat 5710 (January 28, 1950), in his room at 770 Eastern Parkway. He is 69 years old. His passing leaves Chabad without a Rebbe. His son-in-law Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson, who had been directing Merkos and Kehos, becomes the presumptive successor — though he does not accept for nearly a full year.

events.title: Rebbe Mourns and Refuses Leadership for a Year
events.desc: Following the Frierdiker Rebbe's passing, Rabbi Menachem Mendel enters a period of profound mourning and refuses to accept the leadership formally, despite the unanimous wish of the Chabad community. For nearly a year he continues directing Merkos and Kehos but resists assuming the title of Rebbe.

events.title: Rebbe's First Farbrengen as Rebbe
events.desc: The Rebbe holds his first farbrengen (Chassidic gathering) at 770. The farbrengen — an informal gathering with Chassidic teachings, songs, and discussion — becomes the primary vehicle through which he transmits his vision. Over the following decades, thousands of farbrengens are held, each attracting hundreds or thousands of followers.

events.title: The Rebbe Accepts the Nesius (10 Shevat)
events.desc: On 10 Shevat 5711 (January 17, 1951) — exactly one year after the Frierdiker Rebbe's passing — Rabbi Menachem Mendel formally accepts the leadership of Chabad-Lubavitch. He delivers his first maamer as Rebbe: Basi L'Gani, continuing directly from where his father-in-law left off. He declares this generation's mission: to draw divine presence into the world.

events.title: Rebbe's Vision for Jewish Women's Education
events.desc: The Rebbe begins vigorously promoting advanced Jewish education for women, insisting that girls deserve the same quality of Jewish education as boys. He champions the Bais Rivkah girls' school network and writes extensively about the unique spiritual mission of Jewish women, making this one of the defining features of his leadership.

events.title: Rebbe Begins Responding to Science Questions
events.desc: The Rebbe begins writing extensively on the relationship between Torah and modern science — on topics including the age of the universe, evolution, quantum mechanics, and more. His approach is to show that apparent conflicts stem from misunderstanding either Torah or science, and that true science and authentic Torah are always compatible.

events.title: First Summer Camps Established by Chabad
events.desc: The Rebbe spearheads the establishment of Camp Gan Israel, a summer camp network providing Jewish children with an immersive Jewish experience in a camp setting. Camp Gan Israel spreads rapidly worldwide and becomes the largest Jewish summer camp network in the world.

events.title: Rebbe's Letters on Mental Health and Torah
events.desc: The Rebbe begins his extraordinary engagement with questions of mental health, writing detailed letters integrating Torah wisdom with psychological insights. He emphasizes the therapeutic value of mitzvot, the importance of joy, and the dangers of excessive self-analysis. His approach anticipates by decades what becomes 'positive psychology.'

events.title: First Chabad House Opens in Morocco
events.desc: Chabad emissaries establish one of the first formal Chabad centers outside North America in Morocco, where a large Sephardic Jewish community faces enormous challenges of assimilation and emigration. This marks the beginning of Chabad's reach into traditionally non-Ashkenazic Jewish communities.

events.title: Rebbe Meets with David Ben-Gurion
events.desc: Israel's prime minister David Ben-Gurion visits the Rebbe in Brooklyn. In a famous exchange, Ben-Gurion asks who is a Jew (a matter then under debate in Israel). The Rebbe argues powerfully from a principled Jewish legal perspective. Ben-Gurion is reportedly deeply impressed by the Rebbe's logical clarity, though the Israeli government ultimately adopts a different standard.

events.title: Rebbe Establishes Chabad in Australia
events.desc: Chabad emissaries are dispatched to Australia, establishing centers in Melbourne and Sydney. Australian Jewry, largely Sephardic Holocaust survivors, finds in Chabad a vibrant expression of traditional Judaism. Within decades, Chabad becomes the most active Orthodox organization in Australia.

events.title: Rebbe's Sicha on Jewish Education and Public Schools
events.desc: The Rebbe delivers a famous discourse on the importance of Jewish day school education and the dangers of relying solely on public school education for Jewish children. He argues that the minimum Jewish education required is not just Hebrew school but a full-time Jewish educational environment.

events.title: Rebbe Mourns Rebbetzin Chana's Passing
events.desc: The Rebbe's mother, Rebbetzin Chana Schneerson, passes away in Brooklyn. She had escaped the Soviet Union after her husband Rabbi Levi Yitzchak's death in exile and reached America, where she lived near her son. Her passing deeply moves the Rebbe, who speaks of her courage and piety with great reverence.

events.title: Rebbe's Letters on the Uniqueness of Every Jewish Soul
events.desc: The Rebbe writes a series of influential letters articulating his belief that every Jew — regardless of background, level of observance, or personal history — possesses an indestructible divine soul. This theological position underpins the entire Chabad outreach model: no Jew is ever 'too far' to be reached.

events.title: Rebbe Dispatches Shluchim to Soviet Union
events.desc: Despite the Cold War, the Rebbe dispatches emissaries and coordinates the delivery of Jewish materials to Soviet Jews through creative diplomatic channels. Western visitors to the USSR carry in tefillin, matzot, and Jewish books. The operation maintains Jewish consciousness among millions of Jews cut off from their heritage.

events.title: Chabad Establishes Presence at Western Wall
events.desc: Following Israel's capture of the Old City of Jerusalem, Chabad immediately establishes a presence at the newly accessible Western Wall. Chabad rabbis begin offering tefillin and Jewish education to visitors. The Western Wall becomes a major site of Chabad outreach for decades.

events.title: Six Day War — Rebbe's Prediction of Victory
events.desc: In the tense days before Israel's Six Day War (June 1967), while many feared destruction, the Rebbe publicly predicted Israeli victory with certainty, based on divine promises and strategic assessment. His confidence calms the American Jewish community and contributes to the massive tefillin campaign that follows.

events.title: Tefillin Campaign — Half a Million Men Put on Tefillin
events.desc: In the aftermath of the Six Day War, Chabad sets up tefillin stations at army induction centers, airports, and Jewish neighborhoods worldwide. Hundreds of thousands of Jewish men put on tefillin for the first time, many of them secular Israelis and American Jews moved by the miraculous military victory. The campaign becomes Chabad's signature outreach activity.

events.title: First Chabad House on University Campus (UCLA)
events.desc: Rabbi Shlomo Cunin opens the first formal Chabad House on a university campus at UCLA in Los Angeles. The concept — a Chabad couple living in an open home near a university campus, welcoming all Jewish students regardless of background — proves enormously successful and is rapidly replicated at dozens, then hundreds of universities.

events.title: Rebbe Launches 'Mitzvah Tanks'
events.desc: The Rebbe introduces the concept of 'Mitzvah Tanks' — retrofitted vans and trucks that travel through New York City streets, offering Jewish men the opportunity to put on tefillin. The concept spreads to cities worldwide. Mitzvah Tanks become one of the most distinctive images of Chabad outreach.

events.title: Rebbe Sends Shluchim to Behind the Iron Curtain
events.desc: The Rebbe develops increasingly sophisticated systems for maintaining Jewish life in the Soviet Union and Eastern Bloc. Jewish tourists are briefed before traveling to the USSR on how to contact underground Jewish leaders and deliver religious materials. These networks help sustain Jewish identity for millions of Soviet Jews.

events.title: Rebbe's 70th Birthday — Major Celebration at 770
events.desc: The Rebbe's 70th birthday (11 Nissan 5732) is celebrated with an enormous farbrengen at 770, attended by thousands. The Rebbe uses the occasion to launch new initiatives and articulate his vision for the next generation of Chabad leadership and outreach. He emphasizes that each person's 'candle' can light countless other candles.

events.title: Yom Kippur War — Rebbe's Response
events.desc: When Egypt and Syria launch a surprise attack on Israel on Yom Kippur 1973, the Rebbe responds with characteristic confidence, urging calm and predicting Israeli survival. He dispatches emissaries to Israel to maintain morale. After the war, he emphasizes the miraculous elements of Israel's survival and uses the occasion to deepen the tefillin campaign.

events.title: The 'Mivtzoim' — Seven Mitzvah Campaigns Launched
events.desc: The Rebbe formalizes the 'Mivtzoim' (campaigns): Tefillin, Shabbat, Kashrus, Taharat HaMishpacha, Torah study, Mezuzah, and Tzedakah (charity). These seven campaigns provide a framework for Chabad outreach worldwide, giving every emissary a clear, actionable agenda for engaging Jews of all backgrounds.

events.title: Tzivos Hashem — Children's Organization Founded
events.desc: Tzivos Hashem ('Army of God') is established as a children's movement that presents Jewish observance through the metaphor of being a soldier in God's army. Children receive 'ranks' for completing mitzvot. The program engages hundreds of thousands of children worldwide and creates a generation of proud, committed young Jews.

events.title: Rebbe Advocates for Moment of Silence in Public Schools
events.desc: The Rebbe undertakes a sustained campaign to restore voluntary prayer or a moment of silence to American public schools. He argues before Congress, through letters to the President, and through public advocacy. He views this as essential to children's moral and spiritual development, regardless of religion.

events.title: Rebbe Establishes Chabad in South America
events.desc: Major Chabad centers are established throughout South America — in Argentina, Brazil, Uruguay, and other countries. These centers serve large Ashkenazic communities as well as Sephardic Jews, providing education, outreach, and community services in an increasingly secularized Jewish environment.

events.title: Rebbe's Letter on the Inviolability of Jewish Life
events.desc: The Rebbe writes a landmark letter on the sanctity of human life in Judaism, particularly opposing abortion, arguing that every Jewish life from conception carries infinite divine value. This letter influences Jewish bioethical discussions for decades.

events.title: Chabad Opens in China, Japan, and Far East
events.desc: Chabad emissaries are dispatched to East Asia — including Japan, Hong Kong, and eventually mainland China — serving Jewish business travelers and expatriates as well as small permanent Jewish communities. These centers demonstrate Chabad's commitment to reaching every Jew wherever they are found.

events.title: Children's Sefer Torah Campaign
events.desc: The Rebbe launches a campaign for children under Bar/Bat Mitzvah age to acquire a letter in a specially written Torah scroll. Hundreds of thousands of Jewish children worldwide 'purchase' a letter, creating a personal connection to the Torah. The campaign generates unprecedented excitement among Jewish children and families.

events.title: Rebbe's Campaign for Seven Noahide Laws
events.desc: The Rebbe launches a major campaign to promote awareness of the Seven Noahide Laws among non-Jews, arguing this is part of the Jewish mission to bring moral monotheism to humanity. He meets with politicians and dignitaries, and lobbies the U.S. Congress. In 1991, Congress formally recognizes the Seven Noahide Laws as the basis of civilization.

events.title: Rebbe's 'Dollar Distributions' Begin
events.desc: The Rebbe begins the practice of distributing dollar bills to individuals who come to receive a blessing, on Sunday afternoons. Visitors from all walks of life — presidents, paupers, rabbis, celebrities — stand in line for hours to receive a dollar (for charity) and a blessing from the Rebbe. The practice continues for over a decade, with thousands of people each Sunday.

events.title: Rebbe Responds to Lebanon War
events.desc: When Israel invades Lebanon in 1982, the Rebbe expresses concern for the safety of Israeli soldiers while supporting Israel's right to self-defense. He sends special care packages to Israeli troops and dispatches Chabad emissaries to military bases in Israel to provide tefillin and spiritual support.

events.title: Daily Rambam Study Instituted
events.desc: The Rebbe institutes a global daily study schedule for Maimonides' Mishneh Torah. The 1,000+ chapters are divided among Jews worldwide into three tracks: one chapter per day (completing the work in three years), three chapters per day (one year), or the weekly halacha. The program unites Jews of all backgrounds in simultaneous Torah study.

events.title: Likkutei Sichot — 39-Volume Torah Commentary
events.desc: The Rebbe's weekly Torah discourses are systematically compiled and edited into Likkutei Sichot, eventually comprising 39 volumes. This work — covering every weekly Torah portion, every holiday, and countless special topics — becomes the most comprehensive Torah commentary of the 20th century. It is studied daily in thousands of Chabad study groups worldwide.

events.title: Rebbe Establishes Chabad in Sub-Saharan Africa
events.desc: Chabad emissaries are dispatched to sub-Saharan Africa — including South Africa, Zimbabwe, Kenya, and other countries — serving both established Jewish communities and transient Jewish business people. In South Africa, Chabad becomes the most dynamic Orthodox organization during the apartheid and post-apartheid periods.

events.title: Chabad's Soviet Aid Network — Peak Operation
events.desc: By the mid-1980s, Chabad maintains one of the most sophisticated Jewish underground operations in the Soviet Union. Materials flow in through diplomatic pouches, tourist channels, and underground couriers. Secret seders are held for thousands of Jews. Chabad maintains contact with dozens of underground religious leaders.

events.title: Rebbe's Vision for Post-Soviet Jewry
events.desc: The Rebbe begins articulating a vision for massive expansion of Jewish life in the USSR following what he predicts will be its collapse. He instructs young rabbis to prepare themselves for missions to Russia, Ukraine, and the other republics. His prediction of the Soviet Union's fall surprises many who hear it.

events.title: Chabad Surpasses 1,000 Centers Worldwide
events.desc: Chabad-Lubavitch surpasses 1,000 centers and institutions in dozens of countries worldwide, cementing its position as the largest Jewish outreach organization in history. The Rebbe uses the occasion to call for accelerating expansion, saying that every Jew who remains unreached is a tragedy.

events.title: Rebbe Intensifies Moshiach Awareness Campaign
events.desc: Following Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka's passing, the Rebbe greatly intensifies his focus on the imminence of the Messianic era. He teaches that this generation is the 'last generation of exile and first generation of redemption.' The Moshiach campaign transforms Chabad's identity and generates both enormous enthusiasm and considerable controversy.

events.title: Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka Passes Away (22 Shevat)
events.desc: Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka Schneerson passes away on 22 Shevat 5748 (February 10, 1988). The Rebbe is devastated by her passing. He dedicates an unprecedented amount of time to her eulogy and her memory, speaking of her extraordinary qualities and their 60-year partnership. The Rebbe instructs that her name be memorialized through Jewish institutions worldwide.

events.title: Fall of Communism — Rebbe's Vindication
events.desc: As the Berlin Wall falls and the Soviet Union begins to collapse, Chabad's decades of underground preparation pay off spectacularly. The movement is uniquely positioned to rebuild Jewish life in the Former Soviet Union. The Rebbe's earlier predictions of Communism's fall are cited as evidence of his prophetic vision.

events.title: Chabad Explodes Across Former Soviet Union
events.desc: Within months of the Soviet Union's opening, Chabad centers are established in dozens of former Soviet cities. Young rabbis couple the Rebbe trained are dispatched throughout Russia, Ukraine, Belarus, and the other republics. Synagogues are reclaimed, yeshivos opened, mikvaot built, and Jewish schools established at breathtaking speed.

events.title: Rebbe's Response to Gulf War — 'Israel is Safe'
events.desc: During the Gulf War (1990-91), as Saddam Hussein launches Scud missiles at Israel and many Israeli families flee, the Rebbe publicly assures that Israel is the safest place for Jews. He quotes the Talmud's teaching that the Land of Israel is uniquely protected by divine providence. His statement, issued without hedging, is remarkably reassuring to many.

events.title: Operation Exodus — Chabad Aids Soviet Jewish Emigration
events.desc: As hundreds of thousands of Soviet Jews emigrate to Israel and the West, Chabad operates reception centers, provides guidance, and helps Jewish immigrants reconnect with their heritage. The Rebbe personally encourages Soviet Jews to emigrate to Israel and supports the infrastructure to receive them.

events.title: Public Chanukah Menorahs — Global Phenomenon
events.desc: By 1991, public Chanukah menorahs (chanukiot) erected by Chabad appear in city squares, government buildings, and public spaces in hundreds of cities on six continents. The campaign — challenged in some American courts on church-state grounds — wins a landmark Supreme Court case (Pittsburgh case, 1989). It becomes the world's largest annual Jewish public display.

events.title: Rebbe Receives Ellis Island Medal of Honor
events.desc: The Rebbe receives the Ellis Island Medal of Honor, awarded to individuals who have made outstanding contributions to American society while maintaining their heritage. The award recognizes his decades of work in education, human rights, and community service. He donates the medal's cash prize to charity.

events.title: Rebbe's Continuing Presence After Stroke
events.desc: Despite being unable to speak, the Rebbe continues to be present at public gatherings, acknowledging the crowd with his hands and eyes. Videos of these appearances — showing his evident recognition of and connection with his followers — become deeply emotional documents of a leader's unwavering commitment even in physical decline.

events.title: The Rebbe Suffers a Stroke (27 Adar I)
events.desc: On 27 Adar I 5752 (March 2, 1992), the Rebbe suffers a massive stroke at the Ohel (while visiting the Frierdiker Rebbe's resting place). He loses the ability to speak but retains his cognitive awareness. For two years he communicates through nods and hand gestures. The Chabad community is plunged into profound grief and uncertainty.

events.title: US Congress Recognizes Rebbe's 91st Birthday
events.desc: For many years, the U.S. Congress designates 11 Nissan (the Rebbe's birthday) as 'Education and Sharing Day U.S.A.' On the occasion of his 91st birthday in 1993, both the Senate and House pass special resolutions honoring his contributions to education and moral leadership. Similar recognition is extended by legislatures in other countries.

events.title: Chabad Continues Without a New Rebbe
events.desc: Following the Rebbe's passing, Chabad-Lubavitch continues to operate and expand without appointing a new Rebbe — an unprecedented situation in Chassidic history. The movement's thousands of emissaries, united by their devotion to the Rebbe's mission, continue and dramatically accelerate his work.

events.title: The Ohel Becomes World-Famous Pilgrimage Site
events.desc: The Rebbe's resting place in Queens, known as the Ohel, becomes one of the most visited Jewish sites in the world. Open 24 hours a day, 365 days a year, it receives hundreds of thousands of visitors annually from every background — observant and secular, Jewish and non-Jewish, politicians and ordinary people.

events.title: The Rebbe Passes Away (3 Tammuz)
events.desc: Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson passes away on 3 Tammuz 5754 (June 12, 1994) at 1:50 AM, at Beth Israel Hospital in New York. He is 92 years old. Hundreds of thousands mourn worldwide. He is buried at the Ohel in Queens — the resting place of his father-in-law the Frierdiker Rebbe — which becomes one of the most visited Jewish sites in the world.

events.title: Rebbe's Library (Agudas Chasidei Chabad) Legal Victory
events.desc: A landmark legal case over the Rebbe's personal library is resolved in Chabad's favor. The Rebbe had collected thousands of rare books and manuscripts. After his passing, the library becomes the subject of a complex legal dispute, ultimately recognized as communal property belonging to Agudas Chasidei Chabad.

events.title: Rebbe Awarded Congressional Gold Medal Posthumously
events.desc: The United States Congress awards the Lubavitcher Rebbe the Congressional Gold Medal posthumously, the highest civilian honor in the United States. He joins fewer than 200 individuals in American history to receive this recognition. The citation notes his work in education, humanitarian causes, and moral leadership.

events.title: Chabad Establishes 2,000th Center Worldwide
events.desc: Chabad-Lubavitch surpasses 2,000 centers and institutions worldwide. The growth trajectory continues to accelerate as the Rebbe's model proves extraordinarily reproducible: a trained shliach couple, an open home, and unconditional love for every Jew.

events.title: September 11 — Chabad Provides Chaplaincy at Ground Zero
events.desc: Following the September 11 terrorist attacks, Chabad rabbis are among the first chaplains at Ground Zero in New York City. They minister to rescue workers, victims' families, and first responders, providing spiritual support and Jewish burial assistance. Chabad's Lower Manhattan center becomes a hub of relief activity.

events.title: Chabad in Iraq and Afghanistan With U.S. Troops
events.desc: As American forces deploy to Iraq and Afghanistan, Chabad military chaplains and visiting rabbis provide Jewish soldiers with tefillin, matzot, menorahs, and spiritual support. Chabad establishes one of the most active Jewish military chaplaincy programs in U.S. history.

events.title: Chabad in the Tsunami Zone
events.desc: Following the devastating Indian Ocean tsunami of December 2004, Chabad emissaries in Thailand and India are among the first to provide relief to Jewish tourists and locals affected by the disaster. The Chabad House in Koh Samui and other centers transform into relief centers overnight.

events.title: Chabad House in Nepal Becomes Famous Trekkers' Stop
events.desc: The Chabad House in Kathmandu, Nepal — known for its Friday night dinners welcoming hundreds of Israeli trekkers after Himalayan expeditions — becomes internationally famous as a symbol of Chabad's reach to the most remote Jewish travelers. Israeli tourists make it a mandatory stop before and after major treks.

events.title: Mumbai Terror Attack — Chabad House Targeted (26 Cheshvan)
events.desc: On November 26-28, 2008, Islamist terrorists attack multiple sites in Mumbai including Nariman House (the Chabad House). Rabbi Gavriel Noach Holtzberg (29) and Rebbetzin Rivka Holtzberg (28) — along with four other Jewish guests — are murdered. Their two-year-old son Moshe is rescued by his nanny Sandra Samuel. The attack shocks the world; Chabad vows to rebuild.

events.title: New Chabad House Opens in Mumbai — 'We Are Not Afraid'
events.desc: One year after the Mumbai terror attack, a new, expanded Chabad House is inaugurated at the same location in Mumbai. The reopening ceremony — attended by diplomats, Indian officials, and thousands of supporters — sends a powerful message about Chabad's resilience. The slogan 'We Are Not Afraid' becomes a motto for the movement.

events.title: Chabad Responds to Haiti Earthquake
events.desc: Following the catastrophic Haiti earthquake of January 2010, Chabad dispatches emergency medical teams and relief workers to provide aid. Though Haiti has virtually no Jewish population, the Rebbe's teaching that every human being is precious motivates Chabad's involvement in the relief effort.

events.title: Chabad in Cuba — Jewish Community Revived
events.desc: Chabad emissaries play a pivotal role in reviving the remnant Jewish community in Cuba. Working within the constraints of Cuban law, they provide Jewish education, Passover seders, and community services to the island's aging Jewish population, helping maintain Jewish identity in an isolated communist state.

events.title: Kinus HaShluchim Draws 4,000 Rabbis to Brooklyn
events.desc: The annual International Conference of Chabad-Lubavitch Emissaries (Kinus HaShluchim), held each November in Brooklyn, draws over 4,000 rabbis from more than 80 countries. The event — the largest annual gathering of rabbis in history — includes learning sessions, inspiration, and coordination of worldwide activities.

events.title: Chabad Establishes Presence in 100+ Countries
events.desc: Chabad-Lubavitch becomes the first Jewish organization in history to maintain active centers in more than 100 countries simultaneously. This includes centers in North Korea's neighboring states, Central Asian republics, Pacific island nations, and virtually every country in the world with a Jewish presence.

events.title: Chabad Responds to Las Vegas Shooting
events.desc: When a mass shooting at a Las Vegas concert kills 58 and wounds 869, Chabad of Las Vegas transforms into a pastoral care center. Rabbi Shea Harlig and his team minister to victims and first responders around the clock. The response becomes a model of faith community emergency care.

events.title: Pittsburgh Synagogue Shooting — Chabad Responds
events.desc: The Tree of Life Synagogue shooting in Pittsburgh on October 27, 2018 (27 Cheshvan) kills 11 Jewish worshippers in the deadliest antisemitic attack in American history. Chabad's Pittsburgh center (near the attack site) immediately mobilizes pastoral care, and Chabad worldwide organizes solidarity events and memorial services.

events.title: Poway Chabad Synagogue Shooting (Last Day of Passover)
events.desc: On the last day of Passover 5779 (April 27, 2019), a gunman opens fire at the Chabad of Poway synagogue in California during morning services. Lori Gilbert-Kaye is killed; Rabbi Yisroel Goldstein (who lost fingers) and two others are wounded. Rabbi Goldstein's defiant response — continuing to speak from the pulpit with his wounded hand raised — becomes iconic.

events.title: COVID-19 — Chabad Pivots to Virtual Outreach
events.desc: The COVID-19 pandemic forces all Chabad centers to close physically. Within days, thousands of Chabad Houses pivot to virtual programming — Zoom seders, online Shabbat candle-lighting, virtual classes and farbrengens. The crisis demonstrates Chabad's adaptability and the strength of its personal relationships with community members.

events.title: Chabad Delivers Passover Packages During COVID Lockdowns
events.desc: During the 2020 COVID Passover — the first in living memory when Jews worldwide could not hold communal seders — Chabad emissaries deliver matzot, wine, and seder plates to isolated Jewish families in hundreds of cities worldwide. The operation, coordinated in days, serves hundreds of thousands of people.

events.title: Chabad Celebrates 70 Years of the Rebbe's Leadership
events.desc: The Chabad community marks the 70th anniversary of the Rebbe's acceptance of the nesius with worldwide celebrations, exhibitions, and rededications. The occasion prompts reflection on the extraordinary transformation the Rebbe wrought: from a small refugee community in 1950 to a worldwide movement in 2021.

events.title: October 7 Hamas Attack — Chabad Responds in Israel
events.desc: Following the devastating Hamas terror attack on October 7, 2023, in which over 1,200 Israelis are murdered and 253 taken hostage, Chabad centers throughout Israel transform into welfare and support centers. Chabad emissaries visit IDF soldiers, evacuated communities, and hostage families, providing spiritual support and material aid around the clock.

events.title: Annual Matzot Baking Program — Millions Served
events.desc: Chabad's worldwide matzot campaign provides hand-baked matzot, seder plates, and Passover supplies to Jews in hundreds of countries — including many who receive this as their only Jewish connection of the year. The operation, coordinated by Merkos, serves millions of Jews annually across every continent.

events.title: Chabad Operates 5,000+ Centers in 100+ Countries
events.desc: Chabad-Lubavitch now operates more than 5,000 institutions in over 100 countries. It maintains the largest Jewish educational system in the world, the largest network of Jewish day camps, and the most widespread Jewish outreach infrastructure in history. Each year, approximately 100 new emissary families are dispatched to new locations worldwide.
